title: Apply: Judicial Branch
description: Apply your knowledge of the judicial branch and the federal judiciary in this set of multiple-choice questions.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Federalist No. 78 by Alexander Hamilton.**

That inflexible and uniform adherence to the rights of the Constitution, and of individuals, which we perceive to be indispensable in the courts of justice, can certainly not be expected from judges who hold their offices by a temporary [[☃ definition 1]]. [[☃ definition 2]] appointments, however regulated, or by whomsoever made, would, in some way or other, be fatal to their necessary independence.

**Based on the excerpt, which government policy would the author most likely support?**
[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: appointment; the act of being officially given a job
happening again and again at regular intervals

choices.content: Congressional oversight of the judicial branch
A ban on presidential appointments of Supreme Court justices
Life terms, pending good behavior, for Supreme Court justices
Regular democratic elections of Supreme Court justices

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Federalist No. 78 by Alexander Hamilton.**

There is no position which depends on clearer principles, than that every act of a delegated authority, contrary to the [[☃ definition 1]] of the [[☃ definition 2]] under which it is exercised, is void. No legislative act, therefore, contrary to the Constitution, can be valid.

**The viewpoint expressed in this excerpt is consistent with the Supreme Court’s reasoning in which case?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the act of authorizing someone to do something

choices.content: *Marbury v. Madison* (1803)
*McCulloch v. Maryland* (1819)
*Korematsu v. United States* (1942)
*West Virginia v. EPA* (2022)

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Federalist No. 78 by Alexander Hamilton.**

There is no position which depends on clearer principles, than that every act of a delegated authority, contrary to the [[☃ definition 1]] of the [[☃ definition 2]] under which it is exercised, is void. No legislative act, therefore, contrary to the Constitution, can be valid.

**Which statement best summarizes Hamilton’s argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the act of authorizing someone to do something

choices.content: Few legislative acts are contrary to the Constitution.
Laws that violate the Constitution are not valid.
The Constitution allows Congress to delegate its authority to pass legislative acts.
Arguments against the ratification of the Constitution are invalid.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the majority decision in *Marbury v. Madison* (1803), written by Justice John Marshall.**

The [[☃ definition 1]] between a government with limited and unlimited powers is abolished, if those limits do not [[☃ definition 2]] the persons on whom they are imposed....It is a proposition too plain to be contested, that the constitution controls any legislative act [[☃ definition 3]] to it; or, that the legislature may alter the constitution by an ordinary act.

Between these alternatives there is no middle ground. The constitution is either a superior, [[☃ definition 4]] law, unchangeable by ordinary means, or it is on a level with ordinary legislative acts, and, like other acts, is alterable when the legislature shall please to alter it.

**Which statement is most consistent with Marshall’s argument in this passage?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: There is no meaningful distinction between governments with limited and unlimited powers.
The Constitution is above ordinary laws passed by Congress, as it is the supreme law of the land.
The legislature should be able to alter the Constitution by an ordinary act passed by both houses of Congress.
The Constitution should not be changed under any circumstances because it is perfect as written.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the majority decision in *Marbury v. Madison* (1803), written by Justice John Marshall.**

The [[☃ definition 1]] between a government with limited and unlimited powers is abolished, if those limits do not [[☃ definition 2]] the persons on whom they are imposed....It is a proposition too plain to be contested, that the constitution controls any legislative act [[☃ definition 3]] to it; or, that the legislature may alter the constitution by an ordinary act.

Between these alternatives there is no middle ground. The constitution is either a superior, [[☃ definition 4]] law, unchangeable by ordinary means, or it is on a level with ordinary legislative acts, and, like other acts, is alterable when the legislature shall please to alter it.

**The reasoning in this excerpt established which principle?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Judicial review
Separation of powers
Birthright citizenship
Strict scrutiny

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Federalist No. 78 by Alexander Hamilton.**

The complete independence of the courts of justice is peculiarly essential in a limited Constitution. By a limited Constitution, I understand one which contains certain specified exceptions to the legislative authority . . . Limitations of this kind can be preserved in practice no other way than through the medium of courts of justice, whose duty it must be to declare all acts contrary to the [[☃ definition 1]] [[☃ definition 2]] of the Constitution void. Without this, all the reservations of particular rights or privileges would amount to nothing.

**Which statement best summarizes Hamilton’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The judicial branch should not be able to overrule legislative acts because doing so would violate the separation of powers.
The judicial branch needs to be held accountable for its decisions and have term limits to protect the particular rights and privileges of the people.
The judicial branch should defer to Congress on legislative issues because members of Congress are elected, but judges are not.
The judicial branch is responsible for interpreting the Constitution and judging whether laws are constitutional.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from James Wilson’s speech to the Pennsylvania Ratifying Convention in 1787.**

I say, under this Constitution, the legislature may be restrained, and kept within its prescribed bounds, by the [[☃ definition 1]] of the judicial department. . . . For it is possible that the legislature, when acting in that capacity, may [[☃ definition 2]] the bounds assigned to it, and an act may pass, in the usual mode, notwithstanding that [[☃ definition 3]]; but when it comes to be discussed before the judges—when they consider its principles and find it to be [[☃ definition 4]] with the superior power of the Constitution, it is their duty to pronounce it[[☃ definition 5]]. And judges, independent and not obliged to look to every session for a continuance of their salaries, will behave with [[☃ definition 6]] and refuse to the act the [[☃ definition 7]] of judicial authority. 


**Which statement best summarizes Wilson’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: pass beyond a limit or boundary
the violation of a law,  duty, principle

choices.content: The judiciary does not need the power to review laws, as the Constitution explicitly states what powers Congress does and does not have.
The power to create inferior courts gives Congress too much power over the judiciary, leaving the branch vulnerable to congressional power.
The independence of the judiciary allows the branch to exercise  power to check Congress and invalidate laws that violate the Constitution.
The legislative branch is ultimately responsible for creating inferior courts in the judiciary, so as to maintain judicial independence from the states.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from James Wilson’s speech to the Pennsylvania Ratifying Convention in 1787.**

I say, under this Constitution, the legislature may be restrained, and kept within its prescribed bounds, by the [[☃ definition 1]] of the judicial department. . . . For it is possible that the legislature, when acting in that capacity, may [[☃ definition 2]] the bounds assigned to it, and an act may pass, in the usual mode, notwithstanding that [[☃ definition 3]]; but when it comes to be discussed before the judges—when they consider its principles and find it to be [[☃ definition 4]] with the superior power of the Constitution, it is their duty to pronounce it [[☃ definition 5]]. And judges, independent and not obliged to look to every session for a continuance of their salaries, will behave with [[☃ definition 6]] and refuse to the act the [[☃ definition 7]] of judicial authority. 

**Wilson states that when Congress passes a law that is “ incompatible with the superior power of the Constitution, it is [the Supreme Court’s] duty to pronounce it void.” What is the name for the process that Wilson describes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: pass beyond a limit or boundary
the violation of a law,  duty, principle

choices.content: Judicial review
Judicial independence
Majority opinion
Dissenting opinion

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a letter addressed to Richard Henry Lee, a founder and former president of the Continental Congress, and printed in the *Virginia Independent Chronicle* in 1788. The author of the letter used the [[☃ definition 1]] Cassius II. In this letter, Cassius II responds to Lee’s critiques of the U.S. Constitution. Lee argued that Article III gave Congress the power to set up local courts but that if Congress didn’t create those courts, or made the appeal process unfair, people could face oppression.**

But, tell me, sir, does not the Constitution, expressly declare that inferior [[☃ definition 2]] [federal] courts must be established, and that the Supreme Court shall have [[☃ definition 3]] only in disputes between individuals? How, then, can the Supreme Court determine disputes of this [[☃ definition 4]], unless they are first instituted in the inferior court? Can the Supreme Court have, in such cases, [[☃ definition 5]]? You will not, certainly, assert it. 

To determine [[☃ definition 6]], then, are not inferior courts necessary? You will, not certainly, deny it. A moment’s calm reflection must convince every unbiased mind that the number of inferior courts could not properly be adjusted by the Constitution. The outlines of the plan could only be drawn by the [[☃ definition 7]], and the filling of them up has, unavoidably, been left to Congress. The constitutions of the different states have not [[☃ definition 8]] the number of [state] inferior courts, but have left the arrangement of this matter to their respective legislatures. But, let it be remembered that Congress, as it will contain the collected wisdom and patriotism of the states, will, in all human probability regulate the right of appeal, in such a manner, as to distribute equal and impartial justice.

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the power to interpret a decision by a lower court
the right to hear a legal case and issue a decision

choices.content: Lower courts are not necessary, as each state has its own judiciary meant to uphold their state constitutions.
Lower courts in the federal judiciary are necessary to check the constitutionality of decisions issued by the Supreme Court.
Lower courts are not necessary, as the Supreme Court’s authority to hear certain cases is significantly limited.
Lower courts in the federal judiciary are necessary for the Supreme Court to exercise its power to review appealed cases.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a letter addressed to Richard Henry Lee, a founder and former president of the Continental Congress, and printed in the *Virginia Independent Chronicle* in 1788. The author of the letter used the [[☃ definition 1]] Cassius II. In this letter, Cassius II responds to Lee’s critiques of the U.S. Constitution. Lee argued that Article III gave Congress the power to set up local courts but that if Congress didn’t create those courts, or made the appeal process unfair, people could face oppression.**

But, tell me, sir, does not the Constitution, expressly declare that inferior [[☃ definition 2]] [federal] courts must be established, and that the Supreme Court shall have [[☃ definition 3]] only in disputes between individuals? How, then, can the Supreme Court determine disputes of this [[☃ definition 4]], unless they are first instituted in the inferior court? Can the Supreme Court have, in such cases, [[☃ definition 5]]? You will not, certainly, assert it. 

To determine [[☃ definition 6]], then, are not inferior courts necessary? You will, not certainly, deny it. A moment’s calm reflection must convince every unbiased mind that the number of inferior courts could not properly be adjusted by the Constitution. The outlines of the plan could only be drawn by the [[☃ definition 7]], and the filling of them up has, unavoidably, been left to Congress. The constitutions of the different states have not [[☃ definition 8]] the number of [state] inferior courts, but have left the arrangement of this matter to their respective legislatures. But, let it be remembered that Congress, as it will contain the collected wisdom and patriotism of the states, will, in all human probability regulate the right of appeal, in such a manner, as to distribute equal and impartial justice.

**According to the author, why must Congress be responsible for creating lower courts in the federal court system?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the power to interpret a decision by a lower court
the right to hear a legal case and issue a decision

choices.content: If Congress creates lower courts, the lower courts will carry out the will of the federal legislature.
If Congress creates lower courts, the Supreme Court will have cases to hear on appeal, as the Constitution intended.
If Congress appoints judges to lower federal courts, those judges will feel obligated to rule in Congress’s favor.
If Congress doesn’t create lower courts, state legislatures would have to ensure, that lower courts upholding state constitutions.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from an essay printed in the *Connecticut Courant* in 1788. The author used the [[☃ definition 1]] A Landholder V.**

[The federal] courts are not to [[☃ definition 7]] with [[☃ definition 2]] internal policy and will have [[☃ definition 3]] only of those subjects which are placed under the control of a national legislature. It is as necessary [that] there should be courts of law and executive officers, to carry into effect the laws of the nation, as that there be courts and officers to execute the laws made by your state assemblies. There are many reasons why [[☃ definition 4]] decisions ought not to be left to courts instituted by particular states.

A perfect [[☃ definition 5]] must be observed through the whole Union, or jealousy and unrighteousness will take place; and for a uniformity, one judiciary must [[☃ definition 6]] the whole. The inhabitants of one state will not have confidence in judges appointed by the legislature of another state, in which they have no voice. 


**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: a condition in which everything is the same

choices.content: The individual states will never agree to appoint judges in the federal judiciary.
There are too many state constitutions for the federal judiciary to oversee.
The individual states should be in charge of the federal court system to ensure that states’ rights are protected.
There needs to be one single federal judiciary to uphold the Constitution and ensure legal uniformity nationwide.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from an essay printed in the *Connecticut Courant* in 1788. The author used the [[☃ definition 1]] A Landholder V.**

[The federal] courts are not to [[☃ definition 2]] with [[☃ definition 3]] internal policy and will have [[☃ definition 4]] only of those subjects which are placed under the control of a national legislature. It is as necessary [that] there should be courts of law and executive officers, to carry into effect the laws of the nation, as that there be courts and officers to execute the laws made by your state assemblies. There are many reasons why [[☃ definition 5]] decisions ought not to be left to courts instituted by particular states.

A perfect [[☃ definition 6]] must be observed through the whole Union, or jealousy and unrighteousness will take place; and for a uniformity, one judiciary must [[☃ definition 7]] the whole. The inhabitants of one state will not have confidence in judges appointed by the legislature of another state, in which they have no voice. 

**According to the author, what danger would the United States face if there was no federal judiciary?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: a condition in which everything is the same

choices.content: The state courts would be overwhelmed by the number of cases.
The state courts would be able to act independently from the federal government.
The states would not trust the decisions of other state courts.
The states would not have the authority to strike down any laws.
